skill.name: godot-4-migration
skill.description: Guide for migrating Godot 3.x projects to Godot 4, with GDScript 2.0 syntax and API changes.
skill.summary: Migrate Godot 3.x scripts to Godot 4 using focused GDScript 2.0 guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found only prose and GDScript examples. No shell execution, cryptographic operation, system probing, network access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection text was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The detected backticks are Markdown inline code and fenced GDScript examples for Godot migration. They do not define or invoke shell, Ruby, or system commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation with GDScript snippets only. No executable script file, command interpreter call, or user-controlled command construction appears in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts on lines 3 and 18 do not correspond to any cryptographic API, hashing function, cipher, or credential handling. They are migration description text and Godot terminology.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive skill metadata and a Godot Tween migration note. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, encryption, signing, or password storage logic.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert points to a troubleshooting sentence about the Godot Tween type. It does not inspect the host, list files, read environment data, or collect system metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation for a common Godot compiler error. No operating system command, filesystem enumeration, or environment query is present.

content.value_statement: Godot 3 projects often break when scripts meet GDScript 2.0 syntax and API changes. This skill guides focused updates for exports, Tweens, signals, setters, and async code.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to replace Godot 3 keywords with Godot 4 annotations.
Shows how Godot 4 setters and getters differ from setget patterns.
Guides replacement of Tween nodes with create_tween workflows.
Shows migration from string-based signal connections to callables.
Covers typed arrays and await syntax for GDScript 2.0 updates.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for common Tween type errors.
limitations: Does not scan or rewrite a full Godot project automatically.
Does not cover every Godot engine API change.
Does not validate scenes, resources, or editor settings.
Requires the user to provide project context or affected script snippets.

use_cases.title: Upgrade Gameplay Scripts
use_cases.description: Review gameplay scripts that fail after a Godot 4 upgrade and identify syntax patterns that need manual conversion.

use_cases.title: Modernize UI and Animation Code
use_cases.description: Replace old Tween node usage with Godot 4 create_tween patterns for interface and animation behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare Migration Checklists
use_cases.description: Create focused review lists for exports, signals, async code, typed arrays, and property access changes.

prompt_templates.title: Find Basic Migration Issues
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small script
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Godot 3 script for Godot 4 migration issues. Focus on export syntax, onready variables, tool scripts, and yield usage.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Tween Usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Help migrate my Godot 3 Tween node logic to Godot 4. Explain which parts should use create_tween and parallel tween calls.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Signals and Properties
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this script for Godot 4 signal connection and property setter changes. Point out risky string-based connections and setget replacements.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Project Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Godot 3 to Godot 4 migration checklist for my project. Include script syntax, Tweens, signals, typed arrays, and testing priorities.

output_examples.input: A script uses exported variables, onready nodes, and yield timers.
output: The review identifies annotation updates for exported and onready variables.
It explains that yield-based timer waits should become await-based waits.
It separates required changes from optional typing improvements.

output_examples.input: A scene still uses a Tween node for position and fade animations.
output: The response explains that Godot 4 uses a tween object created in code.
It maps sequential and parallel animation intent to the newer Tween workflow.
It notes that explicit Tween typing is usually unnecessary.

output_examples.input: A team needs a migration review checklist before upgrading.
output: The checklist covers annotations, setters, signals, Tweens, typed arrays, and await usage.
It groups issues by script review area for easier team assignment.
It highlights follow-up testing for gameplay, UI, and animation behavior.

best_practices: Provide the Godot 3 code and describe the target Godot 4 behavior.
Review migration changes in small script groups before testing whole scenes.
Use the guidance as a checklist, then run the project in Godot 4.
anti_patterns: Do not assume every deprecated pattern has a direct one-line replacement.
Do not migrate animation code without checking sequencing and parallel behavior.
Do not skip runtime testing after syntax errors are fixed.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically convert a whole project?
faq.answer: No. It provides migration guidance and review patterns for code you share.

faq.question: What Godot versions does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on moving Godot 3.x projects to Godot 4 and GDScript 2.0.

faq.question: Can it help with Tween errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains the shift from Tween nodes to create_tween and related usage changes.

faq.question: Does it cover signal migration?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains callable-based signal connections and discourages old string-based patterns.

faq.question: Will it validate Godot scenes or resources?
faq.answer: No. It does not inspect project files unless you provide relevant snippets or descriptions.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file is documentation only, with no executable command, network, or prompt injection behavior.
